items.q: Did Gandhi's campaigns alone end British rule in India?
items.correct: No - non-violent mass campaigns were crucial, but the Second World War's drain on Britain, the Quit India movement, naval unrest and a Labour government willing to leave all mattered.
items.misconception: Britain left India only because of Gandhi's marches.
items.random_wrong: India bought its independence from Britain with a single payment.
items.why: Single-hero narratives flatten causation; IB answers must weigh leaders against wider economic and international factors.

items.correct: No - while some transfers were negotiated, independence in Algeria, Kenya, Indochina and Angola came through prolonged and brutal armed conflict.
items.misconception: European empires simply handed power over peacefully everywhere after 1945.
items.random_wrong: All colonies became independent on the same day in 1960.
items.why: Students generalise from India or Ghana; the syllabus expects comparison of non-violent and violent routes to independence.

items.q: Did political independence end foreign influence over new states?
items.correct: No - economic dependence, Cold War rivalry and continued ties to former rulers meant many new states had limited room for manoeuvre, a situation often called neo-colonialism.
items.misconception: Once the flag changed, all foreign control over a new nation ended.
items.random_wrong: New states were legally banned from trading with their former rulers.
items.why: The formal/informal empire distinction is central to evaluating what independence actually achieved.

items.correct: Gandhi's philosophy and method of non-violent resistance
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gandhi's philosophy and method of non-violent resistance. “A British tax on salt” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Partition into India and Pakistan, with mass migration and communal violence
items.misconception: A peaceful united federation including Burma
items.why: It's Partition into India and Pakistan, with mass migration and communal violence. “A peaceful united federation including Burma” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Against which colonial power did the Algerian FLN fight its war of independence (1954-62)?
items.why: Correct answer: France. “Britain” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who led Ghana to independence in 1957?
items.why: It's Kwame Nkrumah. “Jomo Kenyatta” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did India gain independence?
items.why: Exact answer: 1947 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
